primary_diagnosis.name: Leaking / ruptured tubal ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum and early hypovolaemia

pathophysiology_summary: A fertilised ovum has implanted outside the uterine cavity, most commonly in the fallopian tube. As the trophoblast erodes the tubal wall the tube distends and bleeds, then leaks or ruptures, releasing blood into the peritoneal cavity (haemoperitoneum). Free intraperitoneal blood irritates the diaphragm, producing referred right shoulder-tip pain, while ongoing concealed haemorrhage causes tachycardia, pallor, diaphoresis, presyncope and — as compensation fails — hypotension and haemorrhagic shock. In a woman of reproductive age with amenorrhoea, any acute pelvic pain with syncopal features must be treated as a ruptured ectopic until a positive beta-hCG and a transvaginal ultrasound (empty uterus, adnexal mass, free pelvic fluid) either confirm it or an intrauterine pregnancy is demonstrated. Definitive management is emergency surgical (usually laparoscopic salpingectomy); a haemodynamically unstable or ruptured ectopic is NOT a candidate for medical (methotrexate) or expectant management.

differential.evidence_basis: Reproductive-age woman with sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain and near-syncope — the combination mandates 'ectopic until proven otherwise', even before the menstrual history is known.
Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain can be torsion; but torsion less often produces haemorrhagic-shock features.
Can cause sudden pelvic pain and, if bleeding, hypovolaemia; distinguished by pregnancy test and absence of amenorrhoea.
Right iliac fossa pain overlaps; anchoring risk — but does not explain amenorrhoea, vaginal bleeding or syncope from blood loss.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the safety-critical move is to keep ruptured ectopic at the top for ANY reproductive-age woman with acute pelvic pain and faintness, not to anchor on appendicitis.

differential.evidence_basis: ~6 weeks amenorrhoea + light dark vaginal bleeding + unilateral pain + presyncope + shoulder-tip pain is the classic ectopic constellation.
Amenorrhoea + bleeding fits, but miscarriage does not usually cause unilateral pain, shoulder-tip pain or haemorrhagic-shock signs from concealed bleeding.
Remains on the list but the reproductive history and syncope shift probability strongly toward ectopic.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Eliciting the missed period and shoulder-tip pain should sharply consolidate the leading diagnosis and trigger emergency escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Shoulder-tip pain + presyncope + pallor/diaphoresis indicate active intraperitoneal bleeding — time-critical surgical emergency.
Acute sudden onset, absence of fever, and haemodynamic instability from blood loss argue against an infective cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage the correct action is escalation to 000, not further remote work-up.

differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed clinically as high-probability; definitive diagnosis requires serum beta-hCG and transvaginal ultrasound in hospital, and emergency surgical management.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm — but cannot safely exclude either; the correct disposition is immediate emergency transfer.

red_flags.description: Presyncope/near-collapse with pallor and diaphoresis — signs of concealed haemorrhage and early hypovolaemic shock
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates ongoing internal bleeding; can progress to cardiovascular collapse within minutes.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fatal haemorrhagic shock if transfer is delayed.

red_flags.description: Right shoulder-tip pain — referred pain from diaphragmatic irritation by free intraperitoneal blood
red_flags.clinical_significance: Strongly suggests haemoperitoneum from a leaking/ruptured ectopic; a key discriminator from benign causes.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciation of active intra-abdominal bleeding and under-triage.

red_flags.description: Approximately 6 weeks amenorrhoea in a reproductive-age woman with acute pelvic pain and bleeding — possible pregnancy not yet tested
red_flags.clinical_significance: Establishes possible pregnancy; acute pelvic pain in early pregnancy is a ruptured ectopic until excluded by hCG + ultrasound.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing the pregnancy possibility is the pivotal diagnostic error that leads to catastrophic delay.

red_flags.description: Peritonism on examination — marked right iliac fossa/suprapubic tenderness with voluntary guarding and rebound (clinician-elicited)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Peritoneal irritation from intraperitoneal blood; corroborates an acute surgical abdomen but cannot be elicited via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not available remotely — reinforces why remote exclusion is impossible and escalation is mandatory.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Failing to ask about the last menstrual period and pregnancy possibility because the patient says her cycle is 'irregular' and she hasn't tested — the single most dangerous omission in this presentation.
Fixing on right iliac fossa pain as appendicitis or gastroenteritis and ignoring the amenorrhoea, vaginal bleeding and syncopal features.
Attributing the pain to a simple ovarian cyst and under-triaging, when the volume-loss signs point to significant bleeding.
Being reassured by a near-normal blood pressure and a normal SpO2; young patients compensate then decompensate abruptly.
Considering outpatient work-up or medical (methotrexate) management — contraindicated in a suspected ruptured/unstable ectopic, which is a surgical emergency.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exact blood pressure and heart rate (source clinician measured BP 100/60, HR 115, RR 22)
Abdominal examination — right iliac fossa tenderness, voluntary guarding, rebound/peritonism
Pelvic examination — cervical motion tenderness and a palpable tender right adnexal mass
Transvaginal ultrasound (empty uterine cavity, complex adnexal mass, free pelvic fluid)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 for an emergency (Priority 1) ambulance immediately; keep the patient lying flat with legs elevated, nil by mouth in anticipation of surgery, and instruct the partner to monitor breathing and consciousness. Do NOT advise self-transport.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low risk of over-calling; the dominant risk is UNDER-action — attributing the pain to a benign cause, waiting for a morning GP appointment, or advising self-transport instead of an ambulance.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RANZCOG / RCOG Green-top Guideline No. 21 — Ectopic Pregnancy
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Any woman of reproductive age with abdominal pain and haemodynamic compromise should be assumed to have a ruptured ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise and resuscitated/transferred for emergency surgery; unstable/ruptured ectopics are not candidates for methotrexate or expectant management.
